senses.gloss: foredawn, especially the last part of the night before daybreak
to eat before fasting at predawn or foredawn, especially in the last part of the night before daybreak
to charm, to enchant, to fascinate, to spellbind, to bewitch, to ensorcel, to curse, to jinx
to hurt one's heart, to restrict one's chest or breathing

etymology.text: From Proto-Semitic *šaḥ(a)r- (“dawn, morning”). Cognate with Akkadian 𒀉𒄘𒍣𒂵 (šērum), Hebrew שַׁחַר (šáḥar, “dawn”) and Ugaritic 𐎌𐎈𐎗 (šḥr, “dawn; tomorrow, the future”); see Canaanite deity Shahar.
